_meta.description: Ancient religions encountered in scripture - their gods, practices, origins, and biblical interactions

religions.name: Sumerian Religion
religions.description: The oldest known religion in Mesopotamia. Sumerians built the first cities (Ur, Uruk, Eridu) and developed cuneiform writing. Abraham came from Ur, where moon-god worship dominated.

origins.mythology: The gods emerged from the primordial sea (Nammu). An (sky) and Ki (earth) were separated. The gods then created humans from clay mixed with divine blood to serve them and do their work.
origins.historical: Developed in city-states between 4000-3000 BC. Each city had a patron deity. Temples called ziggurats served as the god's earthly dwelling. The religion absorbed and influenced later Babylonian beliefs.

pantheon.name: An/Anu
pantheon.role: Sky god, king of the gods

pantheon.name: Enlil
pantheon.role: God of wind, storms, and earth. Chief god in practice

pantheon.name: Enki/Ea
pantheon.role: God of water, wisdom, and creation. Created humans

pantheon.name: Inanna/Ishtar
pantheon.role: Goddess of love, war, and fertility

pantheon.name: Nanna/Sin
pantheon.role: Moon god. Patron deity of Ur

practices.worship: Daily offerings of food and drink to temple statues. The statue was believed to be the god's living presence. Priests bathed, dressed, and fed the images.
practices.temples: Ziggurats - stepped pyramid temples. The god lived in a shrine at the top. Only priests could enter. The Great Ziggurat of Ur still stands today.
practices.priesthood: Hereditary class. Priests (en) managed temple estates, performed rituals. Priestesses served female deities. Temple staff included singers, diviners, and scribes.
practices.divination: Reading omens was central - examining animal livers, watching oil on water, interpreting dreams. The gods communicated through signs.

worldview.cosmos: Three-tiered universe: heaven (An), earth (Ki), and underworld (Kur). The gods decreed fates (nam) that could not be changed.
worldview.afterlife: Bleak underworld called Kur or Irkalla. All the dead went there regardless of how they lived. Ghosts needed food offerings from the living.
worldview.divine_human: Humans were created to serve the gods. The gods needed food and worship. Humans were inferior beings made from clay.

biblical_interactions.summary: Abraham's family lived in Ur of the Chaldeans, center of moon-god worship
The tower resembles Mesopotamian ziggurats in construction and purpose

religions.archaeological_evidence: Ziggurats at Ur, Uruk, and Eridu. Thousands of cuneiform tablets with myths, hymns, and ritual texts. Cylinder seals showing gods and worship scenes. The Royal Tombs of Ur (2600 BC) with evidence of human sacrifice.

religions.name: Babylonian Religion
religions.description: Built on Sumerian foundations but elevated Marduk as supreme god. Babylon became the cultural and religious center of Mesopotamia. The Jews lived in exile here for 70 years.

origins.mythology: In the Enuma Elish creation epic, Marduk defeats the chaos monster Tiamat and creates the world from her body. He creates humans from the blood of a rebel god to serve the gods. Marduk becomes king of the gods as reward for this victory.
origins.historical: Emerged when Babylon rose to power under Hammurabi (1792-1750 BC). Adopted Sumerian gods but made Marduk supreme. The New Babylonian Empire (626-539 BC) under Nebuchadnezzar II rebuilt Babylon as a religious wonder.

pantheon.name: Marduk
pantheon.role: King of the gods, creator deity, patron of Babylon

pantheon.name: Ishtar
pantheon.role: Goddess of love, war, fertility. Called 'Queen of Heaven'

pantheon.name: Nabu
pantheon.role: God of writing and wisdom. Marduk's son

pantheon.name: Shamash
pantheon.role: Sun god, god of justice and truth

pantheon.name: Sin
pantheon.role: Moon god

pantheon.name: Tammuz
pantheon.role: Dying and rising god of vegetation. Ishtar's lover

pantheon.name: Bel
pantheon.role: Title meaning 'Lord' - applied to Marduk

practices.worship: Daily temple rituals with food offerings, hymns, and prayers. Festivals marked the seasons. The akitu (New Year) festival lasted 12 days and renewed the king's authority.
practices.temples: Esagila was Marduk's main temple in Babylon. The ziggurat Etemenanki ('House of the foundation of heaven and earth') may have inspired the Tower of Babel story.
practices.priesthood: Highly organized temple staff. Different ranks for different functions - sacrifice, divination, exorcism, lamentation. Priests underwent purification rituals.
practices.astrology: Babylonians developed sophisticated astronomy. Stars and planets were gods. Celestial events predicted earthly events. This influenced later Greek and Roman astrology.

worldview.cosmos: Created from chaos (Tiamat). The gods established order from disorder. Stars were divine writings revealing the gods' will.
worldview.afterlife: The underworld (Irkalla) ruled by Ereshkigal. A dark, dusty place where the dead eat clay and wear feathers. Proper burial ensured a tolerable existence.
worldview.divine_human: Humans created to serve the gods. The king was the gods' representative. Fate (shimtu) was decreed by the gods and could rarely be changed.

biblical_interactions.summary: Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem and deported the Jews to Babylon
Ezekiel sees women in the Temple mourning the dying god Tammuz
Jewish women in Egypt worshipped Ishtar as 'Queen of Heaven'
Daniel served in Nebuchadnezzar's court but refused to worship Babylonian gods
Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refused to worship Nebuchadnezzar's golden image
Jeremiah prophesies that Bel and Marduk will be shattered

religions.archaeological_evidence: Ishtar Gate (now in Berlin Museum). Ruins of Babylon including Esagila temple and ziggurat foundation. Thousands of cuneiform tablets - the Enuma Elish, Gilgamesh Epic, astronomical records. Nebuchadnezzar's inscriptions.

religions.name: Assyrian Religion
religions.description: A warrior religion that sanctified military conquest. Ashur, the national god, demanded expansion. The Assyrians conquered the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC and scattered the ten tribes.

origins.mythology: Adopted Babylonian myths but replaced Marduk with Ashur in the Enuma Elish. Ashur was the true victor over Tiamat and rightful king of the gods.
origins.historical: Grew from the city-state of Assur on the Tigris. Ashur was originally the city god who became the national deity. As Assyria expanded, Ashur's domain expanded. Military victory proved divine favor.

pantheon.name: Ashur
pantheon.role: National god, king of gods, god of war and empire

pantheon.name: Ishtar of Nineveh
pantheon.role: Goddess of war (emphasized over love aspect)

pantheon.name: Nisroch
pantheon.role: God worshipped by Sennacherib

pantheon.name: Nabu
pantheon.role: God of wisdom and writing

practices.worship: Temple rituals similar to Babylon. Annual festivals. Royal inscriptions recorded conquests as acts of devotion to Ashur.
practices.temples: Major temples at Assur and Nineveh. Sennacherib built elaborate temples in Nineveh after making it the capital.
practices.warfare: War was religious duty. Enemies were enemies of Ashur. Brutal treatment of conquered peoples (impaling, flaying) was depicted on palace walls as devotion to the god.
practices.divination: Extensive omen collections. Kings consulted diviners before campaigns. Liver divination (extispicy) was especially important.

worldview.cosmos: Ashur was supreme, surpassing even the traditional Mesopotamian gods. The universe existed for Assyria's glory.
worldview.divine_human: The king was Ashur's agent on earth. Conquest glorified the god. Rebellious peoples insulted Ashur and deserved destruction.

biblical_interactions.summary: Shalmaneser V and Sargon II conquered Israel and deported the people
Sennacherib besieged Jerusalem but his army was struck down
His sons killed him while he worshipped in the temple of Nisroch
Jonah was sent to preach to Nineveh, the Assyrian capital
Nahum prophesied the destruction of Nineveh

religions.archaeological_evidence: Palace of Sennacherib at Nineveh with extensive reliefs. Lachish siege reliefs (British Museum). Annals of Assyrian kings mentioning Israel and Judah. Sennacherib's prism describing his campaign against Hezekiah. Library of Ashurbanipal with thousands of tablets.

religions.name: Egyptian Religion
religions.description: One of the world's oldest and longest-lasting religions. Built around the Nile's annual flood, the sun's daily journey, and the belief that death was not the end. Israel lived in Egypt 400 years and the Exodus plagues challenged Egyptian gods directly.

origins.mythology: In the beginning was Nun, the primordial waters. From Nun arose Atum (or Ra), who created himself. He sneezed out Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture), who bore Geb (earth) and Nut (sky). Their children were Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys. Different cities had different creation stories centered on their local god.
origins.historical: Developed from prehistoric Nile cultures. Unified under pharaohs around 3100 BC. Local gods were merged as Egypt unified. The religion evolved over 3,000 years but core beliefs persisted.

pantheon.name: Ra/Re
pantheon.role: Sun god, king of the gods, creator

pantheon.name: Osiris
pantheon.role: God of the dead, resurrection, and the afterlife

pantheon.name: Isis
pantheon.role: Goddess of magic, motherhood, protector of the dead

pantheon.name: Horus
pantheon.role: Sky god, divine kingship. Pharaoh was living Horus

pantheon.name: Set/Seth
pantheon.role: God of chaos, desert, storms, foreigners

pantheon.name: Thoth
pantheon.role: God of wisdom, writing, magic, moon

pantheon.name: Hathor
pantheon.role: Goddess of love, beauty, music, motherhood

pantheon.name: Anubis
pantheon.role: God of mummification, guide of the dead

pantheon.name: Khnum
pantheon.role: Ram-headed god who created humans on a potter's wheel

pantheon.name: Apis
pantheon.role: Sacred bull, manifestation of Ptah and later Osiris

pantheon.name: Hapi
pantheon.role: God of the Nile flood

practices.worship: Daily temple rituals. Priests washed, dressed, and fed the god's statue. Public access was limited to festival days. Ordinary Egyptians worshipped at local shrines and household altars.
practices.temples: Massive stone temples (Karnak, Luxor, Abu Simbel). Oriented to astronomical events. The innermost sanctuary housed the god's image in darkness.
practices.priesthood: Hierarchical. High priests held enormous power. Priests served in rotating shifts. Strict purity rules - shaved heads, linen clothes, ritual washing.
practices.mummification: Preserved the body for the afterlife. The ka (life force) and ba (personality) needed the body. A 70-day process of drying, wrapping, and ritual.

worldview.cosmos: The sun god traveled across the sky by day and through the underworld by night, battling chaos. Ma'at (order, truth, justice) opposed isfet (chaos). The pharaoh maintained ma'at on earth.
worldview.afterlife: The most developed in the ancient world. The dead were judged by Osiris. The heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at. Those who passed entered eternal paradise (Field of Reeds). Those who failed were devoured.
worldview.divine_human: The pharaoh was a god on earth - the living Horus. Ordinary people could achieve immortality through proper burial and the favor of the gods.

biblical_interactions.summary: Joseph interpreted Pharaoh's dreams and became second in command
A new Pharaoh enslaved the Israelites
The plagues challenged Egyptian gods: Nile turned blood (Hapi), frogs (Heqet), darkness (Ra), death of firstborn (Pharaoh as god)
Israel made a calf image, possibly influenced by Apis bull worship from Egypt
Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter
Jews fled to Egypt after Jerusalem fell. Jeremiah prophesied there

religions.archaeological_evidence: Pyramids and temples still standing. Thousands of mummies and tomb paintings. Book of the Dead papyri. Rosetta Stone enabled translation of hieroglyphics. Amarna letters mention Canaan. Merneptah Stele (1208 BC) is oldest Egyptian mention of Israel.

religions.name: Canaanite Religion (Baal Worship)
religions.description: The primary religious rival to Israelite worship. Centered on the storm god Baal and the fertility goddess Asherah. The conflict between Yahweh worship and Baal worship runs through Judges and Kings.

origins.mythology: El was the father god who presided over the divine assembly. His wife was Asherah (or Athirat). Baal (meaning 'Lord') was the storm god who defeated the sea god Yam and the death god Mot in cosmic battles. When Baal died each year (dry season), the land withered. When he revived (rainy season), fertility returned.
origins.historical: Known primarily from the Ugaritic texts discovered at Ras Shamra, Syria in 1929. These 14th-century BC tablets reveal Canaanite mythology in detail. The religion was practiced in city-states throughout Canaan before and during Israel's time there.

pantheon.name: El
pantheon.role: Father of the gods, creator, head of the divine council

pantheon.name: Baal
pantheon.role: Storm god, fertility, king of the gods in practice

pantheon.name: Asherah
pantheon.role: Mother goddess, fertility, consort of El (or Baal)

pantheon.name: Ashtoreth/Astarte
pantheon.role: Goddess of love, war, fertility

pantheon.name: Mot
pantheon.role: God of death and the underworld

pantheon.name: Yam
pantheon.role: Sea god, chaos, Baal's enemy

pantheon.name: Dagon
pantheon.role: Grain god (also worshipped by Philistines)

practices.worship: Sacrifices at high places (bamot) - hilltop shrines. Sacred pillars (massebot) and wooden poles (asherim). Animal sacrifice, grain offerings, libations.
practices.temples: Open-air shrines on hills were common. Indoor temples existed in cities. Temples at Hazor and Megiddo have been excavated.
practices.priesthood: Priests and priestesses served the gods. Prophets of Baal and Asherah mentioned in scripture (1Ki.18.19).
practices.fertility_rites: Rituals to ensure rain and crops. Some sources mention ritual prostitution, though scholars debate the extent. The dying and rising of Baal was enacted ritually.

worldview.cosmos: The gods lived on Mount Zaphon (Mount Cassius in Syria). The divine council (like a royal court) made decisions. Baal's victory over chaos (Yam, Mot) maintained world order.
worldview.afterlife: The underworld was Mot's domain. The dead descended to a shadowy existence. Less developed than Egyptian beliefs.
worldview.divine_human: The gods controlled fertility - rain, crops, livestock, children. Worship secured their favor. The gods could be manipulated through proper ritual.

biblical_interactions.summary: Israel abandoned Yahweh and worshipped the Baals and Ashtaroth
Contest on Mount Carmel. Yahweh sent fire; Baal did not answer
Ahab's Phoenician wife brought Baal worship to Israel
Jehu killed the prophets of Baal and demolished the temple
Josiah removed Baal and Asherah objects from the Temple
Gideon tore down his father's Baal altar

religions.archaeological_evidence: Ugaritic texts (Ras Shamra) - primary source for mythology. Canaanite temples at Hazor, Megiddo, Lachish. Bull figurines and Asherah pole remains. Inscriptions mentioning Baal. Kuntillet Ajrud inscription possibly mentions 'Yahweh and his Asherah.'

religions.name: Phoenician Religion
religions.description: Closely related to Canaanite religion but with local variations. The Phoenicians were famous traders and sailors. Jezebel, daughter of the king of Sidon, brought Phoenician Baal worship to Israel.

origins.mythology: Similar to Canaanite myths. Each city had patron gods: Melqart (Tyre), Eshmun (Sidon), Baalat (Byblos). The Greeks identified their gods with Phoenician ones.
origins.historical: Phoenician cities were cultural and trade centers. Their alphabet became the basis for Greek and later Western alphabets. They spread their religion through Mediterranean colonies, including Carthage.

pantheon.name: Melqart
pantheon.role: City god of Tyre, god of the underworld and cycles of vegetation. Called 'Baal of Tyre'

pantheon.name: Astarte
pantheon.role: Goddess of love, fertility, war

pantheon.name: Eshmun
pantheon.role: Healing god, patron of Sidon

pantheon.name: Baal Hammon
pantheon.role: Storm and fertility god (especially in Carthage)

pantheon.name: Tanit
pantheon.role: Goddess at Carthage, consort of Baal Hammon

pantheon.name: Molech/Moloch
pantheon.role: Deity associated with child sacrifice

practices.worship: Temple sacrifices, festivals, processions. Annual celebration of Melqart's death and awakening.
practices.temples: Temples at Tyre, Sidon, Byblos. The temple of Melqart at Tyre was famous in antiquity.
practices.child_sacrifice: Ancient sources (Philo of Byblos, Diodorus Siculus) describe child sacrifice to Baal/Molech. Archaeological evidence from Carthage (tophet burial sites) shows infant remains. This practice is condemned repeatedly in scripture.

worldview.cosmos: Similar to Canaanite beliefs. The gods controlled nature and human affairs.
worldview.afterlife: The underworld (connected to Melqart's descent). Elaborate burial practices.
worldview.divine_human: Proper worship secured divine favor for trade, crops, and fertility.

biblical_interactions.summary: Hiram of Tyre supplied materials and workers for the Temple
Jezebel, daughter of the king of Sidon, promoted Baal worship in Israel
Solomon built shrines for Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians
Israelites forbidden to offer children to Molech
The prophets of Baal and Asherah ate at Jezebel's table

religions.archaeological_evidence: Tophet burial sites in Carthage and Sardinia with urns containing infant bones. Phoenician inscriptions. Temple remains at Byblos. Sarcophagus of King Ahiram with early alphabetic inscription.

religions.name: Philistine Religion
religions.region: Philistia (coastal southern Canaan - Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath)
religions.description: The Philistines were 'Sea Peoples' who settled the coast after Egypt repelled them. They became Israel's main enemy during the period of the Judges and early monarchy. Their god Dagon had temples at Gaza and Ashdod.

origins.mythology: Unknown. The Philistines came from the Aegean world (possibly Crete - 'Caphtor' in the Bible) and likely brought gods with them, later adopting Canaanite deities.
origins.historical: Arrived as part of the Sea Peoples migration around 1175 BC. Settled the five cities of the Philistine Pentapolis. Their material culture shows Aegean origins but quickly absorbed Canaanite elements.

pantheon.name: Dagon
pantheon.role: Chief god, grain and fertility deity
pantheon.symbol: Grain (later tradition says fish, but this is uncertain)

pantheon.name: Baal-zebub
pantheon.role: God of Ekron. Name means 'Lord of the Flies' (possibly a Hebrew insult form of Baal-zebul 'Lord Prince')

pantheon.name: Ashtoreth
pantheon.role: Goddess of fertility (adopted from Canaanites)

practices.worship: Temple worship with sacrifices. Captured objects (like the Ark) were placed in temples as offerings to their gods.
practices.temples: Temples at Gaza (Dagon), Ashdod (Dagon), Beth-shean (Ashtoreth), Ekron (Baal-zebub). Samson destroyed the temple at Gaza.
practices.priesthood: Priests and diviners. The Philistines consulted their diviners about the Ark (1Sa.6.2).

worldview.cosmos: Likely similar to Canaanite beliefs, though little direct evidence survives.
worldview.divine_human: Military victory proved divine favor. Captured weapons and armor were placed in temples.

biblical_interactions.event: Samson and the temple of Dagon
biblical_interactions.summary: Samson pulled down the temple of Dagon at Gaza, killing the Philistines and himself
The Philistines placed the Ark in Dagon's temple. Dagon fell before it
Philistines put Saul's armor in the temple of Ashtoreth
David killed Goliath, who had cursed David by his gods
The Israelite king sent to inquire of the god of Ekron

religions.archaeological_evidence: Philistine pottery showing Aegean styles. Temple remains at Tell Qasile. Ekron inscription naming Philistine kings. Ashkelon excavations. Aegean-style hearths and architecture in early levels.

religions.name: Moabite Religion
religions.region: Moab (east of the Dead Sea, modern Jordan)
religions.description: The Moabites descended from Lot (Gen.19.37). Their national god Chemosh demanded devotion similar to how Yahweh demanded Israel's. The Moabite Stone reveals a worldview parallel to Israel's.

origins.mythology: Unknown in detail. Chemosh was a warrior god similar to Canaanite deities.
origins.historical: Moab appears in Egyptian and Assyrian records. The Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele, 840 BC) is a major source. King Mesha describes Chemosh's anger and favor in terms very similar to biblical language about Yahweh.

pantheon.name: Chemosh
pantheon.role: National god of Moab, warrior deity

pantheon.name: Ashtar-Chemosh
pantheon.role: Possibly a warrior aspect of Chemosh, or a combined deity

practices.worship: Temple sacrifice. High places. The Moabite Stone mentions Chemosh commanding Mesha to take cities and devote them to destruction (herem) - the same practice as Israel's holy war.
practices.temples: High place at Nebo. Temple at Dibon (capital of Moab).
practices.sacrifice: Animal sacrifice. Mesha sacrificed his son on the wall during Israel's siege (2Ki.3.27). Whether this was typical or desperate is debated.

worldview.cosmos: Chemosh controlled Moab's destiny. When Moab was oppressed, it was because Chemosh was angry. Victory came when Chemosh favored them.
worldview.divine_human: The king served Chemosh. National fortune depended on the god's favor.

biblical_interactions.summary: The Moabite king hired Balaam to curse Israel
Ruth left Moab and its gods to follow Naomi and Yahweh
Solomon built a high place for Chemosh near Jerusalem
Moab rebelled against Israel. Mesha sacrificed his son
Josiah defiled the high place Solomon built for Chemosh
Jeremiah prophesies judgment on Moab and Chemosh

religions.archaeological_evidence: Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele) - 840 BC, now in the Louvre. Most important inscription outside the Bible mentioning Israel and describing Moabite religion. Mentions Yahweh, David's house, and Chemosh. Archaeological sites at Dibon, Nebo, Aroer.

religions.name: Ammonite Religion
religions.region: Ammon (east of the Jordan, around modern Amman, Jordan)
religions.description: The Ammonites descended from Lot (Gen.19.38). Their god Molech (or Milcom) was associated with child sacrifice, making Ammonite religion particularly abhorrent to biblical writers.

origins.historical: Ammon appears in Egyptian and Assyrian records. Capital was Rabbah (modern Amman). Limited inscriptions survive.

pantheon.name: Milcom/Molech
pantheon.role: National god of Ammon. The name may mean 'king'
pantheon.symbol: Unknown. Later tradition described a bronze statue with fire

practices.child_sacrifice: Israelites were forbidden to offer children to Molech (Lev.18.21, 20.2-5). The 'passing through the fire' (2Ki.16.3, 2Ki.23.10) likely refers to child sacrifice. The Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna) outside Jerusalem was where this occurred.

worldview.cosmos: Similar to other West Semitic religions.
worldview.divine_human: The king and people served Milcom. National identity tied to the deity.

biblical_interactions.summary: Ammon oppressed Israel. Jephthah defeated them
Solomon built a high place for Molech, the 'abomination of the Ammonites'
King Ahaz 'made his son pass through the fire'
Manasseh 'made his son pass through the fire'
Josiah defiled Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom to stop child sacrifice
Jeremiah prophesies against Ammon and asks why Milcom took Israel's land

religions.archaeological_evidence: Amman Citadel inscription (9th century BC). Ammonite statues and seals. Limited compared to Moab because of less excavation in the area.

religions.name: Edomite Religion
religions.region: Edom (south of the Dead Sea, modern southern Jordan)
religions.description: The Edomites descended from Esau, Jacob's brother. Their territory was mountainous and included Petra. Their god Qos is known from inscriptions but not mentioned by name in scripture.

origins.historical: Edom appears in Egyptian records from the 13th century BC. They controlled important trade routes. After Judah's fall, Edomites moved into southern Judah (later called Idumea).

pantheon.name: Qos
pantheon.role: National god of Edom. Possibly a storm or mountain god

practices.worship: High place worship in the mountains. Limited details survive.
practices.temples: Shrines at Petra and other sites.

worldview.divine_human: Qos protected Edom. Theophoric names (names containing the god's name) like 'Qos-gabr' appear in Edomite inscriptions.

biblical_interactions.summary: Esau is Edom. His descendants settled the hill country of Seir
Edom refused to let Israel pass through their territory
David put garrisons in Edom and the Edomites became servants
Edom revolted against Judah and set up their own king
Amaziah killed 10,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt
Obadiah prophesies against Edom for violence against Judah

religions.archaeological_evidence: Edomite shrine at Horvat Qitmit. Inscriptions mentioning Qos. Seals with theophoric names. Copper mining sites at Timna and Faynan. Later Nabatean Petra built on Edomite territory.

religions.name: Greek Religion
religions.region: Greece, Hellenistic world (Eastern Mediterranean after Alexander)
religions.description: Greek religion spread throughout the Near East after Alexander the Great. The New Testament world was deeply influenced by Greek culture and religion. Paul encountered Greek worship at Athens, Ephesus, and other cities.

origins.mythology: Complex genealogies of gods. The Titans ruled first, then Zeus overthrew his father Kronos. The Olympian gods ruled from Mount Olympus. Greek myths were recorded in Homer, Hesiod, and later poets.
origins.historical: Developed from Bronze Age Mycenaean religion (1600-1100 BC). The city-state (polis) was the primary religious unit. Each city had patron gods and distinctive festivals. Philosophy (from 600 BC onward) began questioning traditional religion.

pantheon.name: Zeus
pantheon.role: King of the gods, sky god, justice

pantheon.name: Hermes
pantheon.role: Messenger god, commerce, travelers, thieves

pantheon.name: Artemis
pantheon.role: Goddess of the hunt, wilderness, childbirth (Ephesian Artemis was a fertility goddess)

pantheon.name: Apollo
pantheon.role: God of light, music, prophecy, healing

pantheon.name: Athena
pantheon.role: Goddess of wisdom, war, crafts

pantheon.name: Dionysus
pantheon.role: God of wine, ecstasy, theater

pantheon.name: The Unknown God
pantheon.role: An altar in Athens to any god they might have missed

practices.worship: Animal sacrifice, libations, prayers. Festivals with processions, music, drama. Home worship at household shrines.
practices.temples: Major temples: Parthenon (Athens), Temple of Artemis at Ephesus (one of Seven Wonders). Temples housed the god's image but worship was outside at the altar.
practices.priesthood: Often part-time civic duty. Priestesses served goddesses. Oracle at Delphi was the most famous prophetic center.
practices.mysteries: Secret initiation rites promising better afterlife. Eleusinian Mysteries, Dionysiac Mysteries, Orphic cults.

worldview.cosmos: The gods were powerful but not all-powerful. Fate (Moira) bound even Zeus. The world was eternal, not created. Philosophy offered alternative cosmologies.
worldview.afterlife: Hades for most souls - a shadowy existence. Elysian Fields for the blessed. Tartarus for the wicked. Mystery religions promised better fates.
worldview.divine_human: Gods interacted with humans, sometimes having children with them (heroes). The gods were like super-humans with the same passions and conflicts.

biblical_interactions.summary: Paul preached at the Areopagus, mentioning their altar to 'The Unknown God'
At Lystra, the crowd called Barnabas 'Zeus' and Paul 'Hermes' after a healing
Silversmiths making Artemis shrines rioted against Paul's preaching
Paul quoted Aratus: 'In him we live and move and have our being'
Paul addresses eating meat sacrificed in pagan temples
The Greek king set up the 'abomination of desolation' in the Temple (167 BC)

religions.archaeological_evidence: Parthenon still standing in Athens. Temple of Artemis foundations at Ephesus. Thousands of Greek inscriptions, sculptures, and temple remains throughout the Mediterranean. Papyri with religious texts.

religions.name: Roman Religion
religions.description: Roman religion combined traditional Roman gods with Greek religion. As Rome conquered the world, it absorbed local cults. Emperor worship became mandatory. Christians who refused to sacrifice to the emperor faced persecution.

origins.mythology: Romans adopted Greek myths, equating their gods: Jupiter = Zeus, Mars = Ares, Venus = Aphrodite, etc. Traditional Roman religion focused more on ritual correctness than mythology.
origins.historical: Developed from early Italian and Etruscan religion. After conquering Greece (146 BC), Romans absorbed Greek culture. Under Augustus (27 BC), religion was reorganized. Emperor worship became central after Augustus was deified.

pantheon.name: Jupiter
pantheon.role: King of gods, sky, justice (= Zeus)

pantheon.name: Mars
pantheon.role: God of war, father of Romulus and Remus

pantheon.name: Diana
pantheon.role: Goddess of the hunt (= Artemis)

pantheon.name: The Divine Augustus
pantheon.role: First deified emperor. Set the pattern for emperor worship

pantheon.name: Various Emperors
pantheon.role: Emperors were deified after death. Some (like Domitian) claimed divine honors while living

practices.worship: Sacrifice, libations, prayers. Public festivals. The state maintained temples and rites. Citizens were expected to participate in civic religion.
practices.temples: Temples to various gods throughout the empire. The Pantheon in Rome was dedicated to all gods.
practices.priesthood: Colleges of priests (pontifices, augurs, Vestal Virgins). The emperor was pontifex maximus (chief priest).
practices.emperor_cult: Temples and altars to the divine emperor throughout the empire. Annual sacrifice was a loyalty test. Refusal was treason.

worldview.cosmos: Similar to Greek views. Roman virtue (pietas, gravitas) emphasized duty to gods, family, and state.
worldview.afterlife: Similar to Greek Hades. Good Romans hoped for the Elysian Fields. Mystery religions (Isis, Mithras) offered alternatives.
worldview.divine_human: The gods protected Rome. Proper ritual maintained divine favor (pax deorum). The emperor mediated between gods and humans.

biblical_interactions.summary: Caesar Augustus decreed a census, bringing Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem
The Roman governor condemned Jesus to crucifixion
Paul invoked his Roman citizenship to appeal to the emperor
Nero blamed Christians for the fire in Rome (64 AD). Peter and Paul traditionally martyred under Nero
The beast (often interpreted as Roman emperor worship) demands worship
Those who do not worship the image are killed

religions.archaeological_evidence: Roman temples throughout the empire. Inscriptions mentioning emperor worship. Coins showing divine emperors. Pliny's letter to Trajan about Christian refusal to sacrifice. Colosseum where Christians were martyred.

religions.name: Persian Religion (Zoroastrianism)
religions.region: Persia (modern Iran) and the Persian Empire
religions.description: The religion founded by Zoroaster (Zarathustra), emphasizing the cosmic battle between good and evil. The Persians freed the Jews from Babylonian exile. Some scholars see Persian influence on later Jewish beliefs about angels, demons, and resurrection.

origins.mythology: Ahura Mazda (Wise Lord) is the supreme good god. Angra Mainyu (Ahriman) is the evil spirit. The world is a battlefield between truth (asha) and lie (druj). Zoroaster received revelations that reformed earlier Iranian religion.
origins.historical: Zoroaster lived sometime between 1500-600 BC (dates debated). By the time of Cyrus the Great (559-530 BC), Zoroastrianism influenced Persian policy. The Achaemenid kings promoted tolerance of local religions while following Ahura Mazda.

pantheon.name: Ahura Mazda
pantheon.role: Supreme god, creator, lord of wisdom and goodness

pantheon.name: Angra Mainyu/Ahriman
pantheon.role: Evil spirit, adversary, source of evil in the world

pantheon.name: Mithra
pantheon.role: God of contracts, light, warfare (later a separate mystery religion)

pantheon.name: Amesha Spentas
pantheon.role: Holy Immortals - seven divine beings serving Ahura Mazda

practices.worship: Fire temples - fire represented truth and purity. Prayers five times daily. No blood sacrifice.
practices.temples: Fire temples where sacred fires were kept burning. The fire was never allowed to go out.
practices.priesthood: Magi - priestly class. The 'wise men' (magi) in Matthew may have been Zoroastrian priests.
practices.purity: Strong emphasis on ritual purity. The dead were exposed (no burial or cremation) because earth and fire were sacred.

worldview.cosmos: Dualistic - good versus evil. History moves toward a final battle where good triumphs. Time is linear, not cyclical.
worldview.afterlife: Individual judgment at death. The soul crosses the Chinvat Bridge. Good souls reach paradise; wicked souls fall to hell. Final resurrection and judgment at end of time.
worldview.divine_human: Humans must choose between truth and lie. Good thoughts, good words, good deeds are central. Human choices matter in the cosmic struggle.

biblical_interactions.summary: Cyrus decreed that the Jews could return and rebuild the Temple
Isaiah calls Cyrus 'his anointed' (mashiach) - the only non-Israelite so titled
Darius confirmed Cyrus's decree and ordered the Temple completed
The story of Esther takes place in the Persian court of Ahasuerus (Xerxes)
Daniel served Darius the Mede and faced the lion's den
Wise men (magi) from the East came seeking the newborn king

religions.archaeological_evidence: Persepolis reliefs showing Ahura Mazda. Behistun inscription of Darius mentioning Ahura Mazda. Avesta texts (Zoroastrian scriptures). Cyrus Cylinder mentioning his policies. Fire temple remains.
